macro_segments.title: Signs of Life in a Moribund Animal (Mesukenet): Defining Movement and Exclusions

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Mesukenet and the Requirement for Signs of Life During Shechita
Shmuel's Broad Definition of Movement as Any Motion Not Resulting from Death
Exclusions from Valid Movement: Things That Happen Naturally as a Result of Death
Mishnaic Case of Hand Extension Without Retraction as Invalid Movement in Small Animals
Inference from the Mishnah: Hand Retraction Without Prior Extension as Valid
Tosefta Challenge: Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Elazar Bar Rabbi Yosi's Exclusive List
Reconciling Rav's Positive List with the Tosefta's Negative List Through Sound Quality and Defecation Type

macro_segments.title: When Must the Sign of Life Appear: Beginning, Middle, or End of Shechita

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's Position: Sign of Life During (Not Before) Shechita, Reframed as Middle Not End
Rava's Strict Position: Sign of Life Must Appear at the End of Shechita
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak's Lenient Position: Sign at Beginning Suffices
Proof from Mishna about Blood Squirting at Night: Sign Valid Regardless of Timing
Gemara Response: Squirting Blood as Potentially Stronger Sign Justifying Beginning Sufficiency
Counter-proof from Mishnah: Rabbi Eliezer's Squirting Standard as Weaker Than Hand/Foot Movement
Talmudic Discussion of Orphan Calf Born at Moment of Mother's Death from Torah
Conclusion: Halacha Follows Rava Requiring Sign of Life at End of Shechita

macro_segments.title: Detailed Requirements for Valid Movement Signs: Variations by Animal Type and Limb

micro_segments.title: Braita Specification: Small Animal Front Limb Requiring Both Extension and Retraction
Braita Specification: Hind Limb Movement Valid in Either Direction for Small Animals
Braita Specification: Large Animal (Cow) Valid with Any Movement in Any Limb
Braita Specification: Bird Requiring Only Wing or Tail Movement
Gemara: All Specifications Implicit in Earlier Mishnah Except Bird Cases

macro_segments.title: Shechita by Non-Jew for Non-Jew: Owner's Intent Regarding Idolatrous Offering

micro_segments.title: Mishnaic Rule: Shechita by Jew for Non-Jew is Valid; Rabbi Eliezer Disputes
Rabbi Eliezer's Reasoning: Normal Non-Jewish Intent Is Idolatrous Offering
Rabbi Yosi's Kal V'Chomer: Only Actor's Intent Matters, Not Owner's Intent
Gemara Analysis: Two Possible Points of Dispute Between Tanna Kamma and Rabbi Eliezer
First Interpretation: Dispute Over Assumption of Non-Jewish Idolatrous Intent
Second Interpretation: Dispute Over Whether Owner's Intent Matters Outside Temple
Conceptual Framework: Thought as Per Se Invalidation vs. Act Definition in Kodashim and Chulin

topical_tags.term: Shmuel vs. Rav on signs of life
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak - beginning of shechita
orphan (yasom) and mother's death at birth
Rabbi Yosi on actor's intent vs. owner's intent
korbanot vs. chulin (Temple offerings vs. non-sacred)
lishmah vs. shelo lishmah (for its sake vs. not for its sake)
